key_insight: Most “novel” crystal generations from current AI models are not truly new structures; they are usually duplicates of training data or simple elemental substitutions of known prototypes.
engineering_relevance: For materials discovery workflows, novelty checks must go beyond generation alone and explicitly filter for duplication and substitution to avoid overestimating search-space expansion.
method: Built a workflow to classify AI-generated crystals as training duplicates, substitution-derived, or unmatched.
Compared outputs from representative generative models against known crystal databases and substitution rules.
Analyzed structural fingerprints to explain where models memorize versus interpolate in structure space.
evidence_signal: Across representative models, 81–92% of chemically valid and metastable generated crystals were either training duplicates or reproducible by substitution, with especially strong duplication in high-symmetry systems.
watch_item: Novelty assessments may still miss edge cases in ambiguous prototype matching, so results depend on the completeness of training data and substitution criteria.
paper_title: Substitution-Based Analysis of Structural Novelty for Generative Models of Materials
